infobox.value: or **KPK** and formerly known as the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") (NWFP), is a [province](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). Located in the [northwestern region](/wiki/Northern_Pakistan "Northern Pakistan") of the country, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa is the fourth largest province of Pakistan by land area and the third-largest province by population. It is bordered by [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") to the south; [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"), and [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") to the east; and [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan") to the north and northeast. It shares an [international border](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") to the west. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has a varied geography of rugged mountain ranges, valleys, rolling foothills, and dense agricultural farms.

**Khyber Pakhtunkhwa**,[[b]](#cite_note-7) commonly abbreviated **KP** or **KPK** and formerly known as the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") (NWFP), is a [province](/wiki/Administrative_units_of_Pakistan "Administrative units of Pakistan") of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). Located in the [northwestern region](/wiki/Northern_Pakistan "Northern Pakistan") of the country, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa is the fourth largest province of Pakistan by land area and the third-largest province by population. It is bordered by [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") to the south; [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"), [Islamabad Capital Territory](/wiki/Islamabad_Capital_Territory "Islamabad Capital Territory"), and [Azad Kashmir](/wiki/Azad_Kashmir "Azad Kashmir") to the east; and [Gilgit-Baltistan](/wiki/Gilgit-Baltistan "Gilgit-Baltistan") to the north and northeast. It shares an [international border](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") to the west. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has a varied geography of rugged mountain ranges, valleys, rolling foothills, and dense agricultural farms.

The history of the present province of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa is characterized by frequent invasions by various empires, largely due to its geographical proximity to the historically important [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass").[[6]](#cite_note-8) It was the site of the ancient [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara"), and was historically a [stronghold of Buddhism](/wiki/Gandharan_Buddhism "Gandharan Buddhism"). [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") became dominant in the region after the 11th-century conquest of the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") kingdom by the [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids"). The predecessor of the present province was constituted in 1901, under the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"), when the North-West Frontier Province was created by bifurcating the northwestern districts of the erstwhile [Punjab Province](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)").

Although it is colloquially known by a variety of other names, the name "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa" was brought into effect for the North-West Frontier Province in April 2010, following the enactment of the [18th Constitutional Amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan"). On 24 May 2018, the [National Assembly of Pakistan](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "National Assembly of Pakistan") voted in favour of the [25th Constitutional Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan"), which merged the [FATA](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") as well as the [Provincially Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Provincially_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Provincially Administered Tribal Areas") into Khyber Pakhtunkhwa.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

While it is the third-largest Pakistani province in terms of both its population and [its economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Economy of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), it is geographically the smallest. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa's share of Pakistan's GDP has historically comprised 10.5%, amounting to over US$ 30 billion.[[8]](#cite_note-T&F-10) The population of the province forms 16.9% of Pakistan's total population and is multiethnic, with the main ethnic groups being the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), [Hindkowans](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans"), [Saraikis](/wiki/Saraiki_people "Saraiki people"), and [Chitralis](/wiki/Kho_people "Kho people"), among others.[[9]](#cite_note-:7-11)[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## Etymology

*Khyber Pakhtunkhwa* means the "[Khyber](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") side of the land of the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns")",[[11]](#cite_note-USDS-13) where the word *Pakhtunkhwa* means "[Land of the Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtunistan "Pashtunistan")",[[12]](#cite_note-14) while according to some scholars, it refers to "Pashtun culture and society".[[13]](#cite_note-15) The province has had various names throughout history. Other names used or proposed for the province include Gandhara, Afghania, Pashtunistan, Pathanistan, Sarhad, Abaseen, Khyber,[[14]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[15]](#cite_note-:5-17)[[16]](#cite_note-RiazKhan-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Khan/Toosi-19) or a combination of names, such as Hazara-Pakhtunkhwa.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)

When the British established it as a province, they called it "North West Frontier Province" (abbreviated as NWFP) due to its relative location being in the northwest of the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire").[[20]](#cite_note-22) After the creation of Pakistan, Pakistan continued with this name but a Pashtun political party, [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party") based in the province demanded that the province name be changed to "Pakhtunkhwa".[[21]](#cite_note-23) Their logic behind that demand was that [Punjabi people](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people"), [Sindhi people](/wiki/Sindhi_people "Sindhi people") and [Baloch people](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") have their provinces named after their ethnicities but that is not the case for [Pashtun people](/wiki/Pashtun_people "Pashtun people").[[22]](#cite_note-Pakhtunkhwadotcom-24)

[Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"), the largest opposition party at the time was ready to change the province's name by supporting the ruling [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") and ANP, in a constitutional amendment but wanted to name the province something which does not carry an exclusively Pashtun identity in it as they argued that there were other minor communities living in the province especially the [Hazarewals](/wiki/Hazarewals "Hazarewals") of the [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") who spoke [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") thus the word *Khyber* was introduced with the name because it is the name of a major [pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") which connects Pakistan to Afghanistan.[[22]](#cite_note-Pakhtunkhwadotcom-24)

### North-West Frontier Province

For over a hundred years after its founding as a province of [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in 1901, it was known as the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province_(1901%E2%80%931955) "North-West Frontier Province (1901–1955)") (abbreviated as NWFP) until 2010 due to its relative location being in the northwest of the nation.[[23]](#cite_note-25) Unofficially, it was known as *Sarhad* ([Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"): سرحد), derived from the province's Urdu name given to it by the Mughals, which means "frontier".

For most of the history of the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP), there were efforts to change its name. The name *Afghania* was proposed first by the founding leaders of the [Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League") in 1933 and was at least partly chosen to represent the first "a" in "P**a**kistan". The need for a change was explained by the man who named Pakistan in his *[Now or Never](/wiki/Pakistan_Declaration "Pakistan Declaration")* pamphlet, [Choudhary Rahmat Ali](/wiki/Choudhary_Rahmat_Ali "Choudhary Rahmat Ali") Khan, as:

> "North-West Frontier Province" is semantically non-descript and socially wrongful. It is non-descript because it merely indicates their geographical situation as a province of old "British India" [which no longer exists]. It is wrongful because it suppresses the social entity of these people. In fact, it suppresses that entity so completely that when composing the name "Pakistan" for our homelands, I had to call the North-West Frontier Province the Afghan Province.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

Suggestions for new names came and went. Although some of the names were ethnically neutral, most proposals emphasised the province's Pashtun ethnic identity. The renaming issue was an emotional one which often crossed party lines and not all supporters of a renaming agreed on the name Pakhtunkhwa.

#### 20th-century proposals

By the late 20th century, President [Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq](/wiki/Muhammad_Zia-ul-Haq "Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq") agreed with [Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Ghaffar_Khan "Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan") to change the name to *Pashtunistan* but he contended that the term Pashtunistan had become controversial and was being politicized by Afghanistan. Ghaffar Khan suggested Pakhtunkhwa, but Zia-ul-Haq asked Ghaffar Khan to suggest an alternative.[[25]](#cite_note-khyber.org-27)

The name Pakhtunkhwa was approved by the democratically elected constitutional assembly of the province in 1997 by majority vote.[[26]](#cite_note-ghazali.net-28) However, the PML (N) parliamentary party of NWFP rejected the ANP demand but called for Prime Minister [Nawaz Sharif](/wiki/Nawaz_Sharif "Nawaz Sharif") to suggest another "non-controversial" name.[[26]](#cite_note-ghazali.net-28) PML (N) members noted that Sarhad was a good name for the province but, if a change was needed, then it should be named Khyber or Abasin.[[26]](#cite_note-ghazali.net-28) The NWFP chief minister, Sardar Mehtab Ahmed Khan, called for a referendum on the issue as a way of determining the name.[[26]](#cite_note-ghazali.net-28) These offers were rejected by the ANP leadership and the ANP withdrew from both the federal and provincial governments.[[26]](#cite_note-ghazali.net-28)

#### 21st-century proposals

The lack of support for a name change by the PML (N) was defended as opposition to the nationalistic politics being pursued by the ANP.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In May 2008, to accommodate a demand by the people of NWFP who voted for the ANP, the PPP proposed that the name of the North-West Frontier Province be changed to Pakhtunkhwa,[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) however the [Muslim League Nawaz](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)") which had considerable support in the [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko")-speaking [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") region of the province announced it might oppose the name change because of it "being on ethnic grounds" because of opposition by its provincial leadership.[[30]](#cite_note-32)

The name *Pakhtunkhwa* was mentioned for the first time in the United Nation's General Assembly by Pakistani President [Asif Zardari](/wiki/Asif_Ali_Zardari "Asif Ali Zardari") on 26 September 2008.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

### Khyber Pakhtunkhwa

The [Pashtun nationalist](/wiki/Pashtun_nationalism "Pashtun nationalism") [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party") based in the province demanded that the province name be changed to "Pakhtunkhwa".[[32]](#cite_note-34) Their logic behind that demand was that [Punjabi people](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people"), [Sindhi people](/wiki/Sindhi_people "Sindhi people") and [Baloch people](/wiki/Baloch_people "Baloch people") have their provinces named after their ethnicities but that is not the case for [Pashtun people](/wiki/Pashtun_people "Pashtun people").[[22]](#cite_note-Pakhtunkhwadotcom-24)

[Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"), the largest opposition party at the time was ready to change the province's name by supporting the ruling [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") and ANP, in a constitutional amendment but wanted to name the province something other than which does not carry only the Pashtun identity in it as they argued that there were other minor communities living in the province especially the [Hazarewals](/wiki/Hazarewals "Hazarewals") of the [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") who spoke [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") thus the word *Khyber* was introduced with the name because it is the name of a major [pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") which connects Pakistan to Afghanistan.[[22]](#cite_note-Pakhtunkhwadotcom-24)

In early 2010, the process of renaming proceeded and the [Pakistani Senate](/wiki/Pakistani_Senate "Pakistani Senate") confirmed the name change to *Khyber Pakhtunkhwa* in the [18th amendment](/wiki/Eighteenth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Eighteenth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan") to the [Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Constitution_of_Pakistan "Constitution of Pakistan") with a unanimous 90 votes on 15 April 2010.[[33]](#cite_note-:3-35)

### Renaming controversy

The name change of the province was met with strong opposition from the people of [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") and protests erupted in the region with wheel and shutter jam strikes. [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad "Abbottabad") became the nerve center of the movement. On 10 April, the [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Police](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa_Police "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Police") fired at unarmed protesters, leaving 7 dead and dozens injured.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Allegedly, the firing was ordered by the coalition government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, led by the [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party").[[35]](#cite_note-37) This is one of the earliest incidents of [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality") in Pakistan in recent years, occurring before the [Model Town Lahore incident](/wiki/2014_Lahore_clash "2014 Lahore clash"), whose FIR has not been registered still today.[[36]](#cite_note-38)

Arif Nizami, former editor of *[The Nation](/wiki/The_Nation_(Pakistani_newspaper) "The Nation (Pakistani newspaper)")*, said, "This has actually opened a Pandora's box, because of Pakistan's very tenuous polity. Now, on one side, there are identity issues and ethnic issues and provincial autonomy issues. The other side is religious issues and terrorism. It's a very explosive situation."[[37]](#cite_note-Brulliard-39)

### Alternative proposed names

Many alternative names were proposed for the province. Many of these were designed to avoid or balance the ethnic connotations of Pakhtunkhwa.[[14]](#cite_note-:4-16)[[15]](#cite_note-:5-17)

#### Gandhara

The name **Gandhāra** was proposed by [Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"), as a neutral name for the province.[[15]](#cite_note-:5-17)[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") was an ancient [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_people "Indo-Aryan people")[[40]](#cite_note-42) civilization centered in the present-day province.[[41]](#cite_note-auto12-43)[[42]](#cite_note-auto-44)[[43]](#cite_note-auto2-45) The core of the region of Gandhara was the [Peshawar valley](/wiki/Peshawar_valley "Peshawar valley") and [Swat valley](/wiki/Swat_valley "Swat valley"), though the cultural influence of "Greater Gandhara" extended across the Indus river to the [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") region in [Potohar Plateau](/wiki/Potohar_Plateau "Potohar Plateau") and westwards into the [Kabul valley](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") in Afghanistan, and northwards up to the [Karakoram](/wiki/Karakoram "Karakoram") range.[[44]](#cite_note-46)[[45]](#cite_note-47)[[46]](#cite_note-48)

It was attested in the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda"),[[47]](#cite_note-Griffith-49)[[48]](#cite_note-Macdonell1997-50) and it was one of the 16 [Mahajanapadas](/wiki/Mahajanapadas "Mahajanapadas") of the [second urbanisation](/wiki/Second_urbanisation "Second urbanisation").[[41]](#cite_note-auto12-43)[[42]](#cite_note-auto-44)[[43]](#cite_note-auto2-45) The region was a major centre for [Greco-Buddhism](/wiki/Greco-Buddhism "Greco-Buddhism") under the [Indo-Greeks](/wiki/Indo-Greek_Kingdom "Indo-Greek Kingdom") and [Gandharan Buddhism](/wiki/Gandharan_Buddhism "Gandharan Buddhism") under later dynasties, including [Indo-Scythians](/wiki/Indo-Scythians "Indo-Scythians"), [Indo-Parthians](/wiki/Indo-Parthians "Indo-Parthians") and [Kushans](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"). Gandhara was also a central location for the spread of Buddhism to Central Asia and East Asia.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

[Gāndhārī](/wiki/Gandhari_language "Gandhari language"), an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") written in [Kharosthi script](/wiki/Kharosthi_script "Kharosthi script"), acted as lingua franca of the region.[[50]](#cite_note-52) Famed for its unique [Gandharan style of art](/wiki/Gandhara_art "Gandhara art") which is influenced by the classical Hellenistic styles, Gandhara attained its height from the 1st century to the 5th century CE under the [Kushan Empire](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), who had their capital at [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") (*[Puruṣapura](/wiki/Puru%E1%B9%A3apura "Puruṣapura")*).

#### Hazara-Pakhtunkhwa

Some [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") residents said that the new name should be Hazara-Pakhtunkhwa (in reference to the [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") where [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko")-speakers are dominant as compared to the Pashto-speakers elsewhere in the province),[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53)[[52]](#cite_note-:1-54) and others said the name should not be changed since the people were accustomed to North-West Frontier Province.[[37]](#cite_note-Brulliard-39)

## History

Main article: [History of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/History_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "History of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

### Early history

During the times of [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") (3300 BCE – 1700 BCE) the Khyber Pass through [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") provided a route to other neighbouring empires and was used by merchants on trade excursions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] From 1500 BCE, [Indo-Iranian peoples](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") started to enter in the region from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") after having passed the Khyber Pass.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-humshehri.org-56)

The region of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara"), which was primarily based in the area of modern-day Khyber Pakhtunkhwa features prominently in the [Rigveda](/wiki/Rigveda "Rigveda") (c. 1500 – c. 1200 BCE),[[55]](#cite_note-Rigveda-57)[[48]](#cite_note-Macdonell1997-50) as well as the Zoroastrian [Avesta](/wiki/Avesta "Avesta"), which mentions it as *Vaēkərəta*, the sixth most beautiful place on earth created by [Ahura Mazda](/wiki/Ahura_Mazda "Ahura Mazda"). It was one of the 16 [Mahajanapadas](/wiki/Mahajanapadas "Mahajanapadas") of [Vedic era](/wiki/Vedic_era "Vedic era").[[41]](#cite_note-auto12-43)[[56]](#cite_note-auto3-58)[[57]](#cite_note-auto22-59) It was the centre of [Vedic](/wiki/Historical_Vedic_Religion "Historical Vedic Religion") and later forms of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"). Gandhara was frequently mentioned in Vedic epics, including [Rig Veda](/wiki/Rig_Veda "Rig Veda"), [Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana") and [Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata"). It was the home of [Gandhari](/wiki/Gandhari_(Mahabharata) "Gandhari (Mahabharata)"), the princess of [Gandhara Kingdom](/wiki/Gandhara_Kingdom "Gandhara Kingdom").[[58]](#cite_note-60)

### Alexander's conquests

In the spring of 327 BC [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") crossed the Hindu Kush and advanced to [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea_(Punjab) "Nicaea (Punjab)"), where Omphis, king of [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila_Tehsil "Taxila Tehsil") and other chiefs joined him. Alexander then dispatched part of his force through the valley of the Kabul River, while he himself advanced into Bajaur and Swat with his light troops.[[59]](#cite_note-IG148-61) [Craterus](/wiki/Craterus "Craterus") was ordered to fortify and repopulate [Arigaion](/wiki/Arigaion "Arigaion"), probably in Bajaur, which its inhabitants had burnt and deserted. Having defeated the Aspasians, from whom he took 40,000 prisoners and 230,000 oxen, Alexander crossed the [Gouraios](/wiki/Gouraios "Gouraios") ([Panjkora](/wiki/Panjkora "Panjkora")) and entered the territory of the [Assakenoi](/wiki/Assakenoi "Assakenoi") and laid siege to Massaga, which he took by storm. Ora and Bazira (possibly Bazar) soon fell. The people of Bazira fled to the rock Aornos, but Alexander made Embolima (possibly [Amb](/wiki/Amb_State "Amb State")) his base, and attacked the rock from there, which was captured after a desperate resistance. Meanwhile, [Peukelaotis](/wiki/Peukelaotis "Peukelaotis") (in [Hashtnagar](/wiki/Hashtnagar "Hashtnagar"), 17 miles (27 km) north-west of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar")) had submitted, and Nicanor, a Macedonian, was appointed satrap of the country west of the Indus.[[60]](#cite_note-IG149-62)

### Mauryan rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Shiva_and_Parvati_BM_OA1939.01-19.17.jpg/250px-Shiva_and_Parvati_BM_OA1939.01-19.17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shiva_and_Parvati_BM_OA1939.01-19.17.jpg)

An ancient statue of [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva") and [Parvati](/wiki/Parvati "Parvati") found in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa.

Mauryan rule began with [Chandragupta Maurya](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya") displacing the [Nanda Empire](/wiki/Nanda_Empire "Nanda Empire"), establishing the Mauryan Empire. A while after, Alexander's general Seleucus had attempted to once again invade the subcontinent from the Khyber pass hoping to take lands that Alexander had conquered, but never fully absorbed into this empire. Seleucus was defeated and the lands of Aria, Arachosia, Gandhara, and Gedrosia were ceded to the Mauryans in exchange for a matrimonial alliance and 500 elephants. With the defeat of the Greeks, the land was once more under Hindu rule.[[61]](#cite_note-63) Chandragupta's son [Bindusara](/wiki/Bindusara "Bindusara") further expanded the empire. However, it was Chandragupta's grandson [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka"), who converted to [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and made it the official state religion in [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and also [Pakhli](/wiki/Pakhli "Pakhli"), the modern Hazara, as evidenced by rock-inscriptions at [Shahbazgarhi](/wiki/Shahbazgarhi "Shahbazgarhi") and [Mansehra](/wiki/Mansehra "Mansehra").[[60]](#cite_note-IG149-62)

After Ashoka's death the [Mauryan](/wiki/Mauryan "Mauryan") empire fell to pieces, just as in the west the [Seleucid](/wiki/Seleucid "Seleucid") power was waning.

### Indo-Greeks

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Buddha-Vajrapani-Herakles.JPG/250px-Buddha-Vajrapani-Herakles.JPG)](/wiki/File:Buddha-Vajrapani-Herakles.JPG)

Greco-Buddhist representation of the Buddha, seated to the left of a depiction of [Vajrapani](/wiki/Vajrapani "Vajrapani") in the guise of the Hellenic god [Heracles](/wiki/Heracles "Heracles").[[62]](#cite_note-64)

The Indo-Greek king [Menander I](/wiki/Menander_I "Menander I") (reigned 155–130 BCE) drove the Greco-Bactrians out of [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and beyond the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush"), becoming king shortly after his victory.

His empire survived him in a fragmented manner until the last independent Greek king, [Strato II](/wiki/Strato_II "Strato II"), disappeared around 10 CE. Around 125 BCE, the Greco-Bactrian king [Heliocles](/wiki/Heliocles "Heliocles"), son of Eucratides, fled from the [Yuezhi](/wiki/Yuezhi "Yuezhi") invasion of Bactria and relocated to Gandhara, pushing the Indo-Greeks east of the [Jhelum River](/wiki/Jhelum_River "Jhelum River"). The last known Indo-Greek ruler was [Theodamas](/wiki/Theodamas "Theodamas"), from the [Bajaur](/wiki/Bajaur "Bajaur") area of Gandhara, mentioned on a 1st-century CE signet ring, bearing the Kharoṣṭhī inscription *"Su Theodamasa"* (*"Su"* was the Greek transliteration of the [Kushan](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire") royal title *"Shau"* ("[Shah](/wiki/Shah "Shah")" or "King")).

It is during this period that the fusion of Hellenistic and South Asian mythological, artistic and religious elements becomes most apparent, especially in the region of Gandhara.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Local Greek rulers still exercised a feeble and precarious power along the borderland, but the last vestige of the Greco-Indian rulers were finished by a people known to the old Chinese as the Yeuh-Chi.[[60]](#cite_note-IG149-62)

### Indo-Scythian Kingdom

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Buner_reliefs_Scythian_bacchanalian_cropped.jpg/250px-Buner_reliefs_Scythian_bacchanalian_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buner_reliefs_Scythian_bacchanalian_cropped.jpg)

One of the [Buner reliefs](/wiki/Buner_reliefs "Buner reliefs") showing Scythian soldiers dancing. [Cleveland Museum of Art](/wiki/Cleveland_Museum_of_Art "Cleveland Museum of Art").

The [Indo-Scythians](/wiki/Indo-Scythians "Indo-Scythians") were descended from the [Sakas](/wiki/Sakas "Sakas") (Scythians) who migrated from Central Asia into South Asia from the middle of the 2nd century BCE to the 1st century BCE. They displaced the Indo-Greeks and ruled a kingdom that stretched from Gandhara to [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura,_Uttar_Pradesh "Mathura, Uttar Pradesh"). The first Indo-Scythian king [Maues](/wiki/Maues "Maues") established [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") hegemony by conquering [Indo-Greek](/wiki/Indo-Greek "Indo-Greek") territories.[[63]](#cite_note-65) The power of the Saka rulers declined after the defeat to [Chandragupta II](/wiki/Chandragupta_II "Chandragupta II") of the [Gupta Empire](/wiki/Gupta_Empire "Gupta Empire") in the 4th century.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

### Indo-Parthian Kingdom

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Takht-e-bahi.jpg/250px-Takht-e-bahi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takht-e-bahi.jpg)

Ancient [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") [Takht-i-Bahi](/wiki/Takht-i-Bahi "Takht-i-Bahi") (a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site")) constructed by the Indo-Parthians.

The [Indo-Parthian Kingdom](/wiki/Indo-Parthian_Kingdom "Indo-Parthian Kingdom") was ruled by the Gondopharid dynasty, named after its first ruler [Gondophares](/wiki/Gondophares "Gondophares"). For most of their history, the leading Gondopharid kings held [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") (in the present [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan") province of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")) as their residence, but during their last few years of existence the capital shifted between [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") and [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). These kings have traditionally been referred to as Indo-Parthians, as their coinage was often inspired by the [Arsacid](/wiki/Arsacid_dynasty_of_Parthia "Arsacid dynasty of Parthia") dynasty, but they probably belonged to a wider groups of [Iranic](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples") tribes who lived east of [Parthia](/wiki/Parthia "Parthia") proper, and there is no evidence that all the kings who assumed the title *Gondophares*, which means "Holder of Glory", were even related.

### Kushan Empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/KanishkaCasket.JPG/250px-KanishkaCasket.JPG)](/wiki/File:KanishkaCasket.JPG)

[Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar")'s [Kanishka stupa](/wiki/Kanishka_stupa "Kanishka stupa") once kept sacred [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") relics in the [Kanishka casket](/wiki/Kanishka_casket "Kanishka casket").

The [Yuezhi](/wiki/Yuezhi "Yuezhi") nomads had driven the [Sakas](/wiki/Sakas "Sakas") from the highlands of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), and were themselves forced southwards by the nomadic [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu"). One group, known as the Kushan, took the lead, and its chief, [Kadphises I](/wiki/Kujula_Kadphises "Kujula Kadphises"), seized vast territories extending south to the [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") valley. His son Kadphises II conquered North-Western India, which he governed through his generals. His immediate successors were the fabled [Hindu kings](/wiki/Hindu_king "Hindu king"): Kanishka, Huvishka, and Vasushka or Vasudeva, of whom the first reigned over a territory which extended as far east as Benares, far south as Malwa, and also including [Bactria](/wiki/Bactria "Bactria") and the Kabul valley.[[60]](#cite_note-IG149-62)[[65]](#cite_note-IG150-67) Their dates are still a matter of dispute, but it is beyond question that they reigned early in the Christian era. To this period may be ascribed the fine statues and bas-reliefs found in [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") and Udyana. Under Huvishka's successor, [Vasushka](/wiki/V%C4%81sishka "Vāsishka"), the dominions of the Kushan kings shrank.[[65]](#cite_note-IG150-67)

### Shahi dynasties

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg/250px-Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Horseman_on_Hindu_Shahi_coinage.jpg)

Horseman on a coin of Spalapati, i.e. the "War-lord" of the [Hindu Shahis](/wiki/Hindu_Shahis "Hindu Shahis"). The headgear has been interpreted as a [turban](/wiki/Turban "Turban").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERehman1976187_and_Pl._V_B."the_horseman_is_shown_wearing_a_turban-like_head-gear_with_a_small_globule_on_the_top"-68)

The [Turk Shahis](/wiki/Turk_Shahis "Turk Shahis") ruled Gandhara until 870, when they were overthrown by the [Hindu Shahis](/wiki/Hindu_Shahis "Hindu Shahis"). The Hindu Shahis are believed to belong to the Uḍi/Oḍi tribe, namely the people of [Oddiyana](/wiki/Oddiyana "Oddiyana") (modern Swat) in Gandhara,[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[68]](#cite_note-70) although they are also variously stated to be Brāhmāns or Kshātriyas.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

The first king Kallar had moved the capital into Udabandhapura from Kabul, in the modern village of [Hund](/wiki/Hund_(village) "Hund (village)") for its new capital.[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-73)[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75) At its zenith, the kingdom stretched over the [Kabul Valley](/wiki/Kabul_Valley "Kabul Valley"), Gandhara and western [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") under [Jayapala](/wiki/Jayapala "Jayapala").[[74]](#cite_note-Wynbrandt2009-76) Jayapala saw a danger in the consolidation of the Ghaznavids and invaded their capital city of [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") both in the reign of [Sebuktigin](/wiki/Sebuktigin "Sebuktigin") and in that of his son [Mahmud](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni"), which initiated the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") Ghaznavid and Hindu Shahi struggles.[[75]](#cite_note-Lewis2-77) Sebuk Tigin, however, defeated him, and he was forced to pay an indemnity.[[75]](#cite_note-Lewis2-77) Jayapala defaulted on the payment and took to the battlefield once more.[[75]](#cite_note-Lewis2-77) Jayapala however, lost control of the entire region between the Kabul Valley and [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River").[[76]](#cite_note-Ferishta-78)

In the year 1001, soon after Sultan Mahmud came to power and was occupied with the [Qarakhanids](/wiki/Qarakhanid "Qarakhanid") north of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush"), Jaipal [attacked Ghazni](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(1001) "Battle of Peshawar (1001)") once more and upon suffering yet another defeat by the powerful Ghaznavid forces, near present-day [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"). After the [Battle of Peshawar](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(1001) "Battle of Peshawar (1001)"), he died because of regretting as his subjects brought disaster and disgrace to the Shahi dynasty.[[75]](#cite_note-Lewis2-77)[[76]](#cite_note-Ferishta-78)

Jayapala was succeeded by his son [Anandapala](/wiki/Anandapala "Anandapala"),[[75]](#cite_note-Lewis2-77) who along with other succeeding generations of the Shahiya dynasty took part in various unsuccessful campaigns against the advancing Ghaznvids but were unsuccessful. The Hindu rulers eventually exiled themselves to the [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") [Siwalik](/wiki/Siwalik "Siwalik") Hills.[[76]](#cite_note-Ferishta-78)

### Ghaznavids

After the battle of Peshawar, Mahmud of Ghazni had secured controlled over southern regions of Pakhtunkhwa. He also (1024 and 1025) raided the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns").[[65]](#cite_note-IG150-67)[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWynbrandt200952–55-79) His descendants reigned till 1179, when [Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor") took Peshawar, making it part of his expanding [Ghurid Empire](/wiki/Ghurid_Empire "Ghurid Empire").

### Delhi sultanate

Following the invasion by the Ghurids, five unrelated heterogeneous dynasties ruled over the Delhi Sultanate sequentially: the [Mamluk dynasty](/wiki/Mamluk_dynasty_(Delhi) "Mamluk dynasty (Delhi)") (1206–1290), the [Khalji dynasty](/wiki/Khalji_dynasty "Khalji dynasty") (1290–1320), the [Tughlaq dynasty](/wiki/Tughlaq_dynasty "Tughlaq dynasty") (1320–1414), the [Sayyid dynasty](/wiki/Sayyid_dynasty "Sayyid dynasty") (1414–1451), and the [Lodi dynasty](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty") (1451–1526).[[78]](#cite_note-sen2-80)

Meanwhile, the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") now appeared as a political factor. At the close of the fourteenth century they were firmly established in their present-day demographics south of Kohat, and in 1451 Bahlol Lodi's accession to the throne of Delhi gave them a dominant position in [Northern India](/wiki/Northern_India "Northern India"). [Yusufzai](/wiki/Yusufzai "Yusufzai") tribes from the Kabul and Jalalabad valleys began migrating to the [Valley of Peshawar](/wiki/Valley_of_Peshawar "Valley of Peshawar") beginning in the 15th century,[[79]](#cite_note-bosworth-81) and displaced the [Swatis](/wiki/Swati_(Pashtun_tribe) "Swati (Pashtun tribe)") of the [Bhittani](/wiki/Bhittani "Bhittani") confederation and [Dilazak](/wiki/Dilazak "Dilazak") Pashtun tribes across the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") to [Hazara Division](/wiki/Hazara_Division "Hazara Division").[[79]](#cite_note-bosworth-81)

### Mughal empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Masjid_Muhabat_Khan.jpg/250px-Masjid_Muhabat_Khan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masjid_Muhabat_Khan.jpg)

Bestowed by [Mohabbat Khan bin Ali Mardan Khan](/wiki/Ali_Mardan_Khan "Ali Mardan Khan") in 1630, the white-marble façade of the [Mohabbat Khan Mosque](/wiki/Mohabbat_Khan_Mosque "Mohabbat Khan Mosque") is one of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar")'s most iconic sights.

Mughal suzerainty over the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region was partially established after Babar, the founder of the Mughal Empire, invaded the region in 1505 CE via the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass"). The Mughal Empire noted the importance of the region as a weak point in their empire's defences,[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82) and determined to hold Peshawar and Kabul at all cost against any threats from the [Uzbek](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks") *[Shaybanids](/wiki/Shaybanid "Shaybanid")*.[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82)

He was forced to retreat westwards to [Kabul](/wiki/Kabul "Kabul") but returned to defeat the Lodis in July 1526, when he captured Peshawar from [Daulat Khan Lodi](/wiki/Daulat_Khan_Lodi "Daulat Khan Lodi"),[[81]](#cite_note-Miers-83) though the region was never considered to be fully subjugated to the Mughals.[[79]](#cite_note-bosworth-81)

Under the reign of Babar's son, [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun"), a direct Mughal rule was briefly challenged with the rise of the Pashtun Emperor, [Sher Shah Suri](/wiki/Sher_Shah_Suri "Sher Shah Suri"), who began construction of the famous [Grand Trunk Road](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Road "Grand Trunk Road") – which links Kabul, Afghanistan with [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") over 2000 miles to the east. Later, local rulers once again pledged loyalty to the Mughal emperor.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Yusufzai](/wiki/Yusufzai "Yusufzai") tribes rose against Mughals during the Yusufzai Revolt of 1667,[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82) and engaged in pitched-battles with Mughal battalions in Peshawar and [Attock](/wiki/Attock "Attock").[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82) [Afridi](/wiki/Afridi "Afridi") tribes resisted Aurangzeb rule during the Afridi Revolt of the 1670s.[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82) The Afridis massacred a Mughal battalion in the [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") in 1672 and shut the pass to lucrative trade routes.[[82]](#cite_note-richards-84) Following another massacre in the winter of 1673, Mughal armies led by Emperor [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") himself regained control of the entire area in 1674,[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82) and enticed tribal leaders with various awards in order to end the rebellion.[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82)

Referred to as the "Father of Pashto Literature" and hailing from the city of [Akora Khattak](/wiki/Akora_Khattak "Akora Khattak"), the warrior-poet [Khushal Khan Khattak](/wiki/Khushal_Khan_Khattak "Khushal Khan Khattak") actively participated in the revolt against the Mughals and became renowned for his poems that celebrated the rebellious Pashtun warriors.[[80]](#cite_note-Richards-82)

On 18 November 1738, Peshawar was captured from the Mughal governor Nawab Nasir Khan by the [Afsharid](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty") armies during the [Persian invasion of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Nader_Shah%27s_invasion_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Nader Shah's invasion of the Mughal Empire") under [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah").[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86)

### Durrani Empire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Bala_Hisar_Fort.jpg/250px-Bala_Hisar_Fort.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bala_Hisar_Fort.jpg)

[Bala Hissar fort](/wiki/Bala_Hissar,_Peshawar "Bala Hissar, Peshawar") in Peshawar. The fort was used as a royal residence for the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire").

The area fell subsequently under the rule of [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani"), founder of the [Durrani Empire](/wiki/Durrani_Empire "Durrani Empire"),[[85]](#cite_note-87) following a grand nine-day long assembly of leaders, known as the *[loya jirga](/wiki/Loya_jirga "Loya jirga")*.[[86]](#cite_note-88) In 1749, the Mughal ruler was induced to cede [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh_Province "Sindh Province"), the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region") and the important trans [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") to Ahmad Shah in order to save his capital from the Durrani attack.[[87]](#cite_note-89) Ahmad Shah invaded the remnants of the Mughal Empire a third time, and then a fourth, consolidating control over the Kashmir and Punjab regions. In 1757, he captured Delhi and sacked Mathura,[[88]](#cite_note-90) but permitted the Mughal dynasty to remain in nominal control of the city as long as the ruler acknowledged Ahmad Shah's suzerainty over Punjab, Sindh, and Kashmir. Leaving his second son [Timur Shah](/wiki/Timur_Shah_Durrani "Timur Shah Durrani") to safeguard his interests, Ahmad Shah left India to return to Afghanistan.

Their rule was interrupted by a brief invasion of the Hindu [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy"), who ruled over the region following the 1758 Battle of Peshawar for eleven months till early 1759 when the Durrani rule was re-established.[[89]](#cite_note-Schofield,_Victoria_2003_page_47-91)

Under the reign of [Timur Shah](/wiki/Timur_Shah_Durrani "Timur Shah Durrani"), the Mughal practice of using Kabul as a summer capital and Peshawar as a winter capital was reintroduced,[[79]](#cite_note-bosworth-81)[[90]](#cite_note-Hanifi2011-92) Peshawar's [Bala Hissar Fort](/wiki/Bala_Hissar,_Peshawar "Bala Hissar, Peshawar") served as the residence of Durrani kings during their winter stay in Peshawar.

[Mahmud Shah Durrani](/wiki/Mahmud_Shah_Durrani "Mahmud Shah Durrani") became king, and quickly sought to seize Peshawar from his half-brother, [Shah Shujah Durrani](/wiki/Shah_Shujah_Durrani "Shah Shujah Durrani").[[91]](#cite_note-UNESCO-93) Shah Shujah was then himself proclaimed king in 1803, and recaptured Peshawar while Mahmud Shah was imprisoned at Bala Hissar fort until his eventual escape.[[91]](#cite_note-UNESCO-93) In 1809, the British sent an emissary to the court of Shah Shujah in Peshawar, marking the first diplomatic meeting between the British and Afghans.[[91]](#cite_note-UNESCO-93) Mahmud Shah allied himself with the *Barakzai* Pashtuns, and amassed an army in 1809, and captured Peshawar from his half-brother, Shah Shujah, establishing Mahmud Shah's second reign,[[91]](#cite_note-UNESCO-93) which lasted under 1818.

### Sikh Empire

[Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") invaded Peshawar in 1818 and captured it from the Durrani Empire. The [Sikh Empire](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire") based in Lahore did not immediately secure direct control of the Peshawar region, but rather paid nominal [tribute](/wiki/Tribute "Tribute") to Jehandad Khan of Khattak, who was nominated by Ranjit Singh to be ruler of the region.

After Ranjit Singh's departure from the region, Khattak's rule was undermined and power seized by Yar Muhammad Khan. In 1823, Ranjit Singh returned to capture Peshawar, and was met by the armies of [Azim Khan](/wiki/Azim_Khan "Azim Khan") at [Nowshera](/wiki/Nowshera,_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Nowshera, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"). Following the Sikh victory at the [Battle of Nowshera](/wiki/Battle_of_Nowshera "Battle of Nowshera"), Ranjit Singh re-captured Peshawar. Rather than re-appointing Jehandad Khan of Khattak, Ranjit Singh selected Yar Muhammad Khan to once again rule the region.

The Sikh Empire annexed the lower parts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region following advances from the armies of [Hari Singh Nalwa](/wiki/Hari_Singh_Nalwa "Hari Singh Nalwa"). An 1835 attempt by [Dost Muhammad Khan](/wiki/Dost_Mohammad_Khan "Dost Mohammad Khan") to re-occupy Peshawar failed when his army declined to engage in combat with the [Dal Khalsa](/wiki/Dal_Khalsa_(Sikh_Empire) "Dal Khalsa (Sikh Empire)"). Dost Muhammad Khan's son, [Mohammad Akbar Khan](/wiki/Wazir_Akbar_Khan "Wazir Akbar Khan") engaged with Sikh forces the [Battle of Jamrud](/wiki/Battle_of_Jamrud "Battle of Jamrud") of 1837, in which prominent sikh commander Hari Singh was killed.

During Sikh rule, an Italian named [Paolo Avitabile](/wiki/Paolo_Avitabile "Paolo Avitabile") was appointed an administrator of Peshawar in 1838, and is remembered for having unleashed a reign of fear there. The city's famous Mahabat Khan, built in 1630 in the Jeweller's Bazaar, was badly damaged and desecrated by the Sikhs, who also rebuilt the Bala Hissar fort during their occupation of Peshawar.

### British Raj

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_%281%29.jpg/250px-The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_(1).jpg)

A colonial era lithograph of the Khyber Pass, made in 1848 by James Rattray.

[British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") defeated the Sikhs during the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") in 1849, and incorporated small parts of the region into the [Province of Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_Province_(British_India) "Punjab Province (British India)"). While Peshawar was the site of a small revolt against British during the [Mutiny of 1857](/wiki/Mutiny_of_1857 "Mutiny of 1857"), local Pashtun tribes throughout the region generally remained neutral or supportive of the British as they detested the Sikhs,[[54]](#cite_note-humshehri.org-56) in contrast to other parts of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") which rose up in revolt against the British. However, British control of parts of the region was routinely challenged by [Wazir](/wiki/Wazir_(Pashtun_tribe) "Wazir (Pashtun tribe)") tribesmen in [Waziristan](/wiki/Waziristan "Waziristan") and other Pashtun tribes, who resisted any foreign occupation until Pakistan was created. By the late 19th century, the official boundaries of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region still had not been defined as the region was still claimed by the Kingdom of Afghanistan. It was only in 1893 The British demarcated the [boundary with Afghanistan](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") under a treaty agreed to by the Afghan king, [Abdur Rahman Khan](/wiki/Abdur_Rahman_Khan "Abdur Rahman Khan"), following the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War").[[92]](#cite_note-LoC-94) Several princely states within the boundaries of the region were allowed to maintain their autonomy under the terms of maintaining friendly ties with the British. As the British war effort during [World War One](/wiki/World_War_One "World War One") demanded the reallocation of resources from British India to the European war fronts, some tribesmen from Afghanistan crossed the Durand Line in 1917 to attack British posts in an attempt to gain territory and weaken the legitimacy of the border. The validity of the Durand Line, however, was re-affirmed in 1919 by the Afghan government with the signing of the [Treaty of Rawalpindi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rawalpindi "Treaty of Rawalpindi"),[[93]](#cite_note-95) which ended the [Third Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Afghan_War "Third Anglo-Afghan War") – a war in which Waziri tribesmen allied themselves with the forces of Afghanistan's King [Amanullah](/wiki/Amanullah "Amanullah") in their resistance to British rule. The Wazirs and other tribes, taking advantage of instability on the frontier, [continued to resist British occupation until 1920](/wiki/Waziristan_campaign_(1919%E2%80%9320) "Waziristan campaign (1919–20)") – even after Afghanistan had signed a peace treaty with the British.

British campaigns to subdue tribesmen along the Durand Line, as well as three Anglo-Afghan wars, made travel between Afghanistan and the densely populated heartlands of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa increasingly difficult. The two regions were largely isolated from one another from the start of the Second Anglo-Afghan War in 1878 until the start of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") in 1939 when conflict along the Afghan frontier largely dissipated. Concurrently, the British continued their large public works projects in the region, and extended the [Great Indian Peninsula Railway](/wiki/Great_Indian_Peninsula_Railway "Great Indian Peninsula Railway") into the region, which connected the modern Khyber Pakhtunkhwa region to the plains of India to the east. Other projects, such as the [Attock Bridge](/wiki/Attock_Bridge "Attock Bridge"), [Islamia College University](/wiki/Islamia_College_University "Islamia College University"), [Khyber Railway](/wiki/Khyber_train_safari "Khyber train safari"), and establishment of [cantonments](/wiki/Cantonments "Cantonments") in [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat "Kohat"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan "Mardan"), and [Nowshera](/wiki/Nowshera_Cantonment "Nowshera Cantonment") further cemented British rule in the region. In 1901, the British carved out the northwest portions of Punjab Province to create the Northwest Frontier Province (NWFP), which was renamed "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa" in 2010.

During this period, North-West Frontier Province was a "scene of repeated outrages on Hindus."[[94]](#cite_note-96) During the independence period there was a [Congress](/wiki/Congress_party_of_India "Congress party of India")-led ministry in the province, which was led by secular [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") leaders, including Bacha Khan, who preferred joining [India](/wiki/India "India") instead of Pakistan. The [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") Pashtun leadership was also of the view that if joining India was not an option then they should espouse the cause of an independent ethnic Pashtun state rather than Pakistan.[[95]](#cite_note-97) In June 1947, [Mirzali Khan](/wiki/Mirzali_Khan "Mirzali Khan"), [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Bacha_Khan "Bacha Khan"), and other [Khudai Khidmatgars](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgars "Khudai Khidmatgars") declared the [Bannu Resolution](/wiki/Bannu_Resolution "Bannu Resolution"), demanding that the Pashtuns be given a choice to have an independent state of Pashtunistan composing all Pashtun majority territories of British India, instead of being made to join the new state of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"). However, the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") refused to comply with the demand of this resolution, as their departure from the region required regions under their control to choose either to join India or Pakistan, with no third option.[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99) By 1947 Pashtun nationalists were advocating for a united India, and no prominent voices advocated for a union with Afghanistan.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101)

The secular stance of Bacha Khan had driven a wedge between the [ulama](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama") of the otherwise pro-[Congress](/wiki/Congress_party_of_India "Congress party of India") (and pro-Indian unity) [Jamiat Ulema Hind](/wiki/Jamiat_Ulema-e-Hind "Jamiat Ulema-e-Hind") (JUH) and Bacha Khan's [Khudai Khidmatgars](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Badshah_Khan.jpg/250px-Badshah_Khan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Badshah_Khan.jpg)

[Bacha Khan](/wiki/Bacha_Khan "Bacha Khan") with [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi")

There were other tensions in the area as well, particularly those that involved agitations by Pashtun tribesmen against the Imperial government. For example, in 1936, a British Indian court ruled against the marriage of a Hindu girl allegedly converted to Islam in Bannu, after the girl's family filed a case of abduction and forced conversion.[[100]](#cite_note-Yousef_Aboul-Enein-102) The ruling was based on the fact that the girl was a minor and was asked to make her decision of conversion and marriage after she reaches the age of majority, till then she was asked to live with a third party.[[100]](#cite_note-Yousef_Aboul-Enein-102) After the girl's family filed a case, the court ruled in the family's favour, angering the local Muslims who had later gone on to lead attacks against the [Bannu Brigade](/wiki/Bannu_Brigade "Bannu Brigade").[[100]](#cite_note-Yousef_Aboul-Enein-102)

Such controversies stirred up anti-Hindu sentiments amongst the province's Muslim population.[[101]](#cite_note-103) By 1947 the majority of the ulama in the province began supporting the [Muslim League](/wiki/All-India_Muslim_League "All-India Muslim League")'s idea of Pakistan.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

Immediately prior to 1947 [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), the British held a [referendum in the NWFP](/wiki/1947_North-West_Frontier_Province_referendum "1947 North-West Frontier Province referendum") to allow voters to choose between joining India or Pakistan. The polling began on 6 July 1947 and the referendum results were made public on 20 July 1947. According to the official results, there were 572,798 registered voters, out of which 289,244 (99.02%) votes were cast in favour of Pakistan, while 2,874 (0.98%) were cast in favour of India. The Muslim League declared the results as valid since over half of all eligible voters backed the merger with Pakistan.[[103]](#cite_note-:2-105)

The then [Chief Minister](/wiki/List_of_Chief_Ministers_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "List of Chief Ministers of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") [Dr. Khan Sahib](/wiki/Khan_Abdul_Jabbar_Khan "Khan Abdul Jabbar Khan"), along with his brother [Bacha Khan](/wiki/Bacha_Khan "Bacha Khan") and the [Khudai Khidmatgars](/wiki/Khudai_Khidmatgar "Khudai Khidmatgar"), boycotted the referendum, citing that it did not have the options of the NWFP becoming independent or joining Afghanistan.[[104]](#cite_note-Dust_of_Empire-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107)

Their appeal for boycott had an effect, as according to an estimate, the total turnout for the referendum was 15% lower than the total turnout in the 1946 elections,[[106]](#cite_note-108) although over half of all eligible voters backed merger with Pakistan.[[103]](#cite_note-:2-105)

Bacha Khan pledged allegiance to the new state of Pakistan in 1947, and thereafter abandoned his goals of an independent Pashtunistan and a united India in favour of supporting increased autonomy for the NWFP within Pakistan.[[54]](#cite_note-humshehri.org-56) He was subsequently arrested several times for his opposition to the strong centralized rule.[[107]](#cite_note-109) He later claimed that "Pashtunistan was never a reality". The idea of Pashtunistan never helped Pashtuns and it only caused suffering for them. He further claimed that the "successive governments of Afghanistan only exploited the idea for their own political goals".[[108]](#cite_note-110)

### Post-independence

There had been tensions between Pakistan and Afghanistan ever since Afghanistan voted against Pakistan's inclusion in the United Nations in 1948.[[109]](#cite_note-111) After the creation of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") in 1947, Afghanistan was the sole member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") to vote against Pakistan's accession to the UN because of Kabul's claim to the Pashtun territories on the Pakistani side of the [Durand Line](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line").[[110]](#cite_note-pakafgh-112) Afghanistan's [loya jirga](/wiki/Loya_jirga "Loya jirga") of 1949 declared the Durand Line invalid. This led to border tensions with Pakistan. Afghanistan's governments have periodically refused to recognize Pakistan's inheritance of British treaties regarding the region.[[110]](#cite_note-pakafgh-112) As had been agreed to by the Afghan governments following the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War"),[[111]](#cite_note-113) and after the treaty ending [Third Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Afghan_War "Third Anglo-Afghan War"),[[112]](#cite_note-114) no option was available to cede the territory to the Afghans, even though Afghanistan continued to claim the entire region as it was part of the Durrani Empire prior the conquest of the region by the Sikhs in 1818.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

During the 1950s, Afghanistan supported the [Pushtunistan](/wiki/Pushtunistan "Pushtunistan") Movement, a secessionist movement that failed to gain substantial support amongst the tribes of the North-West Frontier Province. Afghanistan's refusal to recognize the Durrand Line, and its subsequent support for the Pashtunistan Movement has been cited as the main cause of tensions between the two countries that have existed since Pakistan's independence.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

After the Afghan-Soviet War, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has become one of the areas of top focus for the [War against Terror](/wiki/War_on_terror "War on terror"). The province has been reported to struggle with the issues of crumbling schools, non-existent healthcare, and lack of any sound infrastructure while areas such as Islamabad and Rawalpindi receive priority funding.[[115]](#cite_note-117)

In 2010, the name of the province changed to "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa". Protests arose among the locals of the Hazara division due to this name change, as they began to demand their own province.[[116]](#cite_note-:0b-118) Seven people were killed and 100 injured in protests on 11 April 2011.[[116]](#cite_note-:0b-118)

The [Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Provincial_Assembly_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") approved a bill on 28 May 2018 to merge the [FATA](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") as well as the [Provincially Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Provincially_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Provincially Administered Tribal Areas") into Khyber Pakhtunkhwa following the approval of the [Twenty-fifth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_Constitution_of_Pakistan "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the Constitution of Pakistan") by the national legislature;[[117]](#cite_note-:0a-119) it was signed into law on 31 May by then [President of Pakistan](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan") [Mamnoon Hussain](/wiki/Mamnoon_Hussain "Mamnoon Hussain"), which officially completed the administrative merger process.[[52]](#cite_note-:1-54)[[118]](#cite_note-120)

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/River_Swat_Pakistan_3.jpg/250px-River_Swat_Pakistan_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:River_Swat_Pakistan_3.jpg)

Northern parts of the province feature forests and dramatic mountain scenery, as in [Swat District](/wiki/Swat_District "Swat District").

Khyber Pakhtunkhwa sits primarily on the [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau") and comprises the junction where the slopes of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") mountains on the [Eurasian Plate](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") give way to the Indus-watered hills approaching [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). This situation has led to seismic activity in the past.[[119]](#cite_note-121) The famous [Khyber Pass](/wiki/Khyber_Pass "Khyber Pass") links the province to Afghanistan, while the [Kohalla Bridge](/wiki/Kohalla_Bridge "Kohalla Bridge") in [Circle Bakote](/wiki/Circle_Bakote "Circle Bakote") Abbottabad is a major crossing point over the [Jhelum River](/wiki/Jhelum_River "Jhelum River") in the east.

Geographically the province could be divided into two zones: the northern zone extending from the ranges of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") to the borders of the Peshawar basin and the southern zone extending from Peshawar to the Derajat basin.

The northern zone is cold and snowy in winters with heavy rainfall and pleasant summers with the exception of the Peshawar basin, which is hot in summer and cold in winter. It has moderate rainfall.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The southern zone is arid with hot summers and relatively cold winters and scanty rainfall.[[120]](#cite_note-122) The Sheikh Badin Hills, a spur of clay and [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") hills that stretch east from the Sulaiman Mountains to the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River"), separates [Dera Ismail Khan District](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") from the *Marwat* plains of the [Lakki Marwat](/wiki/Lakki_Marwat_District "Lakki Marwat District"). The highest peak in the range is the limestone Sheikh Badin Mountain, which is protected by the [Sheikh Badin National Park](/wiki/Sheikh_Badin_National_Park "Sheikh Badin National Park"). Near the Indus River, the terminus of the Sheikh Badin Hills is a spur of [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") hills known as the *Kafir Kot* hills, where the ancient Hindu complex of [Kafir Kot](/wiki/Kafir_Kot "Kafir Kot") is located.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

The major rivers that criss-cross the province are Kabul, Swat, Chitral, Kunar, Siran, Panjkora, Bara, Kurram, Dor, Haroo, Gomal, and Zhob.

Its snow-capped peaks and lush green valleys of unusual beauty have enormous potential for tourism.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

### Climate

The [climate](/wiki/Climate "Climate") of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa varies immensely for a region of its size, encompassing most of the many climate types found in Pakistan. The province stretching southwards from the [Baroghil Pass](/wiki/Baroghil_Pass "Baroghil Pass") in the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") covers almost six degrees of latitude; it is mainly a mountainous region. [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan "Dera Ismail Khan") is one of the hottest places in South Asia while in the mountains to the north the weather is mild in the summer and intensely cold in the winter. The air is generally very dry; consequently, the daily and annual range of temperature is quite large.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

Rainfall also varies widely. Although large parts of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa are typically dry, the province also contains the wettest parts of Pakistan in its eastern fringe especially in [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") season from mid-June to mid-September.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Ghabral_swatvalley_x004.JPG/250px-Ghabral_swatvalley_x004.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ghabral_swatvalley_x004.JPG)

[Ghabral](/wiki/Ghabral "Ghabral"), [Swat Valley](/wiki/Swat_Valley "Swat Valley")

#### Upper and Lower Chitral Districts

[Upper Chitral District](/wiki/Upper_Chitral_District "Upper Chitral District") and [Lower Chitral District](/wiki/Lower_Chitral_District "Lower Chitral District"), due to their location, are completely sheltered from the [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon") that controls the weather in eastern Pakistan, owing to its relatively westerly location and the shielding effect of the [Nanga Parbat](/wiki/Nanga_Parbat "Nanga Parbat") massif. In many ways, they have more in common regarding climate with [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") than South Asia.[[124]](#cite_note-Trekking-126) The winters are generally cold even in the valleys, and heavy [snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") during the winter blocks passes and isolates the region. In the valleys, however, summers can be hotter than on the windward side of the mountains due to lower cloud cover: Chitral can reach 40 °C (104 °F) frequently during this period.[[125]](#cite_note-127) However, the humidity is extremely low during these hot spells and, as a result, the summer climate is less torrid than in the rest of the Indian subcontinent.

Most precipitation falls as thunderstorms or snow during winter and spring, so that the climate at the lowest elevations is [classed as](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") (*Csa*), [continental Mediterranean](/wiki/Continental_Mediterranean_climate "Continental Mediterranean climate") (*Dsa*) or [semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate") (*BSk*). Summers are extremely dry in the north of Chitral district and receive only a little rain in the south around [Drosh](/wiki/Drosh "Drosh").

At elevations above 5,000 metres (16,400 ft), as much as a third of the snow which feeds the large Karakoram and Hindukush [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") comes from the monsoon since these elevations are too high to be shielded from its moisture.[[124]](#cite_note-Trekking-126)

#### Central Khyber Pakhtunkhwa

On the southern flanks of [Nanga Parbat](/wiki/Nanga_Parbat "Nanga Parbat") and in [Upper](/wiki/Upper_Dir_District "Upper Dir District") and [Lower Dir Districts](/wiki/Lower_Dir_District "Lower Dir District"), rainfall is much heavier than further north because moist winds from the [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea") are able to penetrate the region. When they collide with the mountain slopes, winter depressions provide heavy precipitation. The [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon"), although short, is generally powerful. As a result, the southern slopes of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa are the wettest part of Pakistan. Annual rainfall ranges from around 500 millimetres (20 in) in the most sheltered areas to as much as 1,750 millimetres (69 in) in parts of Abbottabad and Mansehra Districts.

This region's climate is classed at lower elevations as [humid subtropical](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa* in the west; *Cwa* in the east); whilst at higher elevations with a southerly aspect, it becomes classed as [humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*). However, accurate data for altitudes above 2,000 metres (6,560 ft) are practically nonexistent here, in Chitral, or in the south of the province.

The seasonality of rainfall in central Khyber Pakhtunkhwa shows very marked gradients from east to west. At Dir, March remains the wettest month due to frequent frontal cloud bands, whereas in Hazara more than half the rainfall comes from the monsoon.[[128]](#cite_note-Wernsted-130) This creates a unique situation characterized by a bimodal rainfall regime, which extends into the southern part of the province described below.[[128]](#cite_note-Wernsted-130)

Since cold air from the [Siberian High](/wiki/Siberian_High "Siberian High") loses its chilling capacity upon crossing the vast Karakoram and Himalaya ranges, winters in central Khyber Pakhtunkhwa are somewhat milder than in Chitral. [Snow](/wiki/Snow "Snow") remains very frequent at high altitudes but rarely lasts long on the ground in the major towns and agricultural valleys. Outside of winter, temperatures in central Khyber Pakhtunkhwa are not so hot as in Chitral. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Significantly higher humidity when the monsoon is active means that heat discomfort can be greater. However, even during the most humid periods the high altitudes typically allow for some relief from the heat overnight.[[129]](#cite_note-131)

#### Southern Khyber Pakhtunkhwa

As one moves further away from the foothills of the Himalaya and Karakoram ranges, the climate changes from the humid subtropical climate of the foothills to the typically [arid climate](/wiki/Arid_climate "Arid climate") of [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), [Balochistan](/wiki/Balochistan,_Pakistan "Balochistan, Pakistan") and southern [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"). As in central Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, the seasonality of precipitation shows a very sharp gradient from west to east, but the whole region very rarely receives significant monsoon rainfall. Even at high elevations, annual rainfall is less than 400 millimetres (16 in) and in some places as little as 200 millimetres (8 in).

Temperatures in southern Khyber Pakhtunkhwa are extremely hot: Dera Ismail Khan in the southernmost district of the province is known as one of the hottest places in the world with temperatures known to have reached 50 °C (122 °F).[[130]](#cite_note-132) In the cooler months, nights can be cold and frosts remain frequent; snow is very rare, and daytime temperatures remain comfortably warm with abundant sunshine.

The province is home to 16.9 percent of [Pakistan's total population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Pakistan "Demographics of Pakistan"). It had a population of 40.9 million at the time of the [2023 Census of Pakistan](/wiki/2023_Census_of_Pakistan "2023 Census of Pakistan"). About 85% of the population lived in rural areas.[[152]](#cite_note-pbs.gov.pk-154)

As per a 2025 *[Dawn News](/wiki/Dawn_News "Dawn News")* report, based on data compiled by research organisation [Population Council](/wiki/Population_Council "Population Council"), [UK Aid](/wiki/Department_for_International_Development "Department for International Development") and the [United Nations Population](/wiki/United_Nations_Population_Division "United Nations Population Division"), Khyber Pakhtunkhwa had an annual growth rate of 2.38%. According to demographic projections, if the province’s current [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of 4 children per woman remains unchanged, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa's population could reach approximately 78 million by 2050.[[153]](#cite_note-DawnPop-155)

### Ethnicity

The largest ethnic group are the [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), who form majority in twenty-six out of the thirty-five districts (at the time of 2023 census).[[154]](#cite_note-156) It has been estimated that up to one-third of the province's population is non-Pashtun,[[155]](#cite_note-157) mainly concentrated in the northern areas. [Hindkowans](/wiki/Hindkowans "Hindkowans") are the second largest ethnic group in the province, mainly settled in the [Hazara region](/wiki/Hazara_region "Hazara region") in northeast and being in majority in the districts of [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad_District "Abbottabad District"), [Haripur](/wiki/Haripur_District "Haripur District") and [Mansehra](/wiki/Mansehra_District "Mansehra District"), where they are known as [Hazarewals](/wiki/Hazarewals "Hazarewals"). They also form a significant urban population in the cities of [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") and [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat "Kohat"), although their historical influence has weakened in recent decades due to the [rural to urban migration](/wiki/Rural_flight "Rural flight") and the influx of [Afghan refugees](/wiki/Afghan_refugees_in_Pakistan "Afghan refugees in Pakistan").[[156]](#cite_note-158)

[Kohistani](/wiki/Kohistani_languages "Kohistani languages") (mainly the speakers of [Maiya](/wiki/Indus_Kohistani "Indus Kohistani") and [Shina](/wiki/Kohistani_Shina "Kohistani Shina")) and [Kho people](/wiki/Kho_people "Kho people") are the dominant ethnic groups in [Kohistan](/wiki/Kohistan_District,_Pakistan "Kohistan District, Pakistan") and [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral_District "Chitral District"), respectively, while the southern district of [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") has a [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_people "Saraiki people") majority.[[157]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-159) Other notable minority ethnic groups include [Torwali people](/wiki/Torwali_people "Torwali people") in the [Bahrain](/wiki/Behrain_Tehsil "Behrain Tehsil") Valley of Swat, [Gawri people](/wiki/Gawri_people "Gawri people") in the valleys of [Kalam](/wiki/Kalam_Valley "Kalam Valley") and [Kumrat](/wiki/Kumrat_Valley "Kumrat Valley"), and the well-known [Kalash people](/wiki/Kalash_people "Kalash people") in the [Kalasha Valleys](/wiki/Kalasha_Valleys "Kalasha Valleys") of Chitral.

Around 1.5 million [Afghan refugees](/wiki/Afghans_in_Pakistan "Afghans in Pakistan") also remain in the province,[[158]](#cite_note-160) the majority of whom are Pashtuns. Despite having lived in the province for over two decades, they are registered as [citizens of Afghanistan](/wiki/Demographics_of_Afghanistan "Demographics of Afghanistan").[[159]](#cite_note-161)

The Pashtuns of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa observe tribal code of conduct called [Pashtunwali](/wiki/Pashtunwali "Pashtunwali") which has four high value components called *nang* (honour), *badal* (revenge), *melmastiya* (hospitality) and *nanawata* (rights to refuge).[[8]](#cite_note-T&F-10)

### Language

Languages of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (2023)[[157]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-159)

1. [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") (81.0%)
2. [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") (9.39%)
3. [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language") (3.17%)
4. [Kohistani](/wiki/Indus_Kohistani_language "Indus Kohistani language") (2.45%)
5. [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (0.64%)
6. [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_Language "Punjabi Language") (0.24%)
7. [Shina](/wiki/Shina_language "Shina language") (0.17%)
8. Others (mainly [Khowar](/wiki/Khowar "Khowar")) (2.94%)

According to the [2023 census](/wiki/2023_Pakistani_census "2023 Pakistani census"), the most widely spoken language is [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto"), native to 81% of the population and spoken throughout the province.[[157]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-159) Other languages with significant numbers of speakers include [Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko") (9.39%), [Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language") (3.17%) and [Kohistani languages](/wiki/Kohistani_languages "Kohistani languages") (2.45%).[[157]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-159) Hindko is primarily spoken in the [Hazara Division](/wiki/Hazara_Division "Hazara Division") in the northeast, and Saraiki-speakers are found in [Dera Ismail Khan District](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District") in the far south of the province.[[9]](#cite_note-:7-11) [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"), being the national and official language, serves as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") for inter-ethnic communications, and sometimes [Pashto](/wiki/Pashto "Pashto") and Urdu are the second and third languages among communities that speak other ethnic languages.[[8]](#cite_note-T&F-10) Kohistani languages is an umbrella term encompassing several languages spoken in the north of the province, including [Indus Kohistani](/wiki/Indus_Kohistani "Indus Kohistani"), [Bateri](/wiki/Bateri_language "Bateri language"), [Chilisso](/wiki/Chilisso_language "Chilisso language"), [Gawri](/wiki/Gawri_language "Gawri language"), [Gawro](/wiki/Gowro_language "Gowro language"), [Torwali](/wiki/Torwali_language "Torwali language"), and [Mankiyali](/wiki/Mankiyali_language "Mankiyali language").[[160]](#cite_note-162)[[161]](#cite_note-163) Around 1 million of the population selected "Other" category, largely referring to [Khowar](/wiki/Khowar_language "Khowar language") in the mountainous northwest [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral "Chitral").[[157]](#cite_note-2023censuslanguage-159)[[9]](#cite_note-:7-11)

In 2011 the provincial government approved in principle the introduction of Pashto, Saraiki, Hindko, Khowar and Kohistani as compulsory subjects for schools in the areas where they are spoken.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

### Religion

See also: [Islam in Pakistan](/wiki/Islam_in_Pakistan "Islam in Pakistan") and [Hinduism in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Hinduism in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

The overwhelming majority of the residents of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa follows and professes the [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") while there is a significant amount of shia Muslims in areas such as Kurram, Kohat, Hangu, Orakzai, Dera Ismail khan, Mardan, and many other districts throughout central-southern kpk. Apart from Twelver Shias there are [Isma'ilis](/wiki/Ismaili_Islam "Ismaili Islam") in the [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral "Chitral") district.[[165]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Pakistan-170) The tribe of [Kalasha](/wiki/Kalash_people "Kalash people") in southern [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral "Chitral") still retain an ancient form of [Polytheism](/wiki/Polytheism "Polytheism") mixed with [Animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), a faith once dominant in the mountainous upper northeast of the district.[[165]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Pakistan-170) There are very small numbers of residents who are the adherents of [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Pakistan "Catholic Church in Pakistan") [denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Pakistan "Hinduism in Pakistan") and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), mainly living in Peshawar and other urban centres.[[166]](#cite_note-171)[[167]](#cite_note-172)
