Both administrative divisions would ultimately merge to form Khyber Pakhtunkhwa in 2018. | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

## Government and politics

### Political leanings and the legislative branch

Main article: [Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Provincial_Assembly_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

The [Provincial Assembly](/wiki/Provincial_Assembly_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") is a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") legislature, which consists of 145 members elected to serve for a constitutionally bounded term of five years. Historically, the province perceived to be a stronghold of the [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party") (ANP); a [pro-Russian](/wiki/Pro-Russian "Pro-Russian"), by [procommunist](/wiki/Procommunism "Procommunism"), [left-wing](/wiki/Socialism_in_Pakistan "Socialism in Pakistan") and nationalist party.[[172]](#cite_note-Rug_Pandits,_Yasir-183)[[173]](#cite_note-Rug_Pandits,_Yasir_Sheikh-184) Since the 1970s, the [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") (PPP) also enjoyed considerable support in the province due to its [socialist agenda](/wiki/Socialism_in_Pakistan "Socialism in Pakistan").[[172]](#cite_note-Rug_Pandits,_Yasir-183) Khyber Pakhtunkhwa was thought to be another leftist region of the country after [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh").[[173]](#cite_note-Rug_Pandits,_Yasir_Sheikh-184)

After the nationwide [general elections](/wiki/2002_Pakistani_general_election "2002 Pakistani general election") held in 2002, a [plurality voting](/wiki/Plurality_voting "Plurality voting") [swing](/wiki/Swing_(politics) "Swing (politics)") in the province elected one of Pakistan's only religiously based provincial governments led by the [ultra-conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Islam "Conservative Islam") [Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal](/wiki/Muttahida_Majlis-e-Amal "Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal") (MMA) during the administration of President [Pervez Musharraf](/wiki/Pervez_Musharraf "Pervez Musharraf"). The [American involvement](/wiki/United_States_invasion_of_Afghanistan "United States invasion of Afghanistan") in neighbouring [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") contributed towards the electoral victory of the Islamic coalition led by [Jamaat-e-Islami Pakistan](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami_Pakistan "Jamaat-e-Islami Pakistan") (JeI) whose social policies made the province a ground-swell of [anti-Americanism](/wiki/Anti-American_sentiment_in_Pakistan "Anti-American sentiment in Pakistan").[[174]](#cite_note-Time-185) The electoral victory of MMA was also in context of [guided democracy](/wiki/Guided_democracy "Guided democracy") in the Musharraff administration that barred the mainstream political parties, the leftist [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") and the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right") [Pakistan Muslim League (N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)") (PML(N)), whose chairmen and presidents having been barred from participation in the elections.[[175]](#cite_note-186)

Policy enforcement of a range of social restrictions, though the implementation of strict [Shariah](/wiki/Shariah "Shariah") was introduced by the Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal government. But the law was never fully enacted due to objections of the [Governor of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Governor_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Governor of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") backed by the [Musharraff administration](/wiki/President_Pervez_Musharaff "President Pervez Musharaff").[[174]](#cite_note-Time-185) Restrictions on public musical performances were introduced, as well as a ban prohibiting music to be played in public places as part of the "Prohibition of Dancing and Music Bill, 2005" – which led to the creation of a thriving underground music scene in Peshawar.[[176]](#cite_note-187) The Islamist government also attempted to enforce compulsory *[hijab](/wiki/Hijab "Hijab")* on women,[[177]](#cite_note-Clarke-188) and wished to enforce gender segregation in the province's educational institutions.[[177]](#cite_note-Clarke-188) The coalition further tried to prohibit male doctors from performing ultrasounds on women,[[177]](#cite_note-Clarke-188) and tried to close the province's cinemas.[[177]](#cite_note-Clarke-188) In 2005, the coalition successfully passed the "Prohibition of Use of Women in Photograph Bill, 2005," leading to the removal of all public advertisements that featured women.[[178]](#cite_note-189)

At the height of [Taliban insurgency in Pakistan](/wiki/Taliban_insurgency_in_Pakistan "Taliban insurgency in Pakistan"), the religious coalition lost its grip in the [general elections held in 2008](/wiki/2008_Pakistani_general_election "2008 Pakistani general election"), and the religious coalition was swept out of power by the leftist [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party") which also witnessed the resignation of President Musharraf in 2008.[[174]](#cite_note-Time-185) The ANP government eventually led the initiatives to repeal the major Islamist's social programs, with the backing of the [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Pakistan "Government of Pakistan") led by [PPP](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party") in [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad").[[179]](#cite_note-190) Public disapproval of [ANP](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party")'s leftist program integrated in [civil administration](/wiki/Government_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") with the sounded allegations of corruption as well as popular opposition against [religious program](/wiki/Religious_Conservatism "Religious Conservatism") promoted by the [MMA](/wiki/Muttahida_Majlis-e-Amal "Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal") swiftly shifted the province's leniency away from the left in 2012.[[172]](#cite_note-Rug_Pandits,_Yasir-183) In 2013, the provincial politics shifted towards [populism](/wiki/Populism "Populism") and [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") when the PTI, led by [Imran Khan](/wiki/Imran_Khan "Imran Khan"), was able to form the [minority government](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") in coalition with the [JeI](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami "Jamaat-e-Islami"); the province now serves as the stronghold of the PTI and is perceived as one of the more right wing areas of the country.[[180]](#cite_note-rugpundits,_Yasir-191) After the [2018 election](/wiki/2018_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa_provincial_election "2018 Khyber Pakhtunkhwa provincial election"), PTI increased their seat share and formed a majority government.

In non-Pashtun areas, such as [Abbottabad](/wiki/Abbottabad_District "Abbottabad District"), and [Hazara Division](/wiki/Hazara_Division "Hazara Division"), the [PML(N)](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League_(N) "Pakistan Muslim League (N)"), the [centre-right](/wiki/Centre-right "Centre-right") party, enjoys considerable public support over economical and public policy issues and has a substantial vote bank.[[180]](#cite_note-rugpundits,_Yasir-191)

### Executive branch

Main article: [Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Government_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

The [executive branch](/wiki/Government_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") of the Kyber Pakhtunkhwa is led by the [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Chief Minister of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") elected by popular vote in the [Provincial assembly](/wiki/Provincial_Assembly_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Assembly of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")[[181]](#cite_note-192) while the Governor, a ceremonial figure representing the [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Pakistan "Government of Pakistan") in Islamabad, is appointed from the necessary advice of the [Prime Minister of Pakistan](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Pakistan "Prime Minister of Pakistan") by the [President of Pakistan](/wiki/President_of_Pakistan "President of Pakistan").[[182]](#cite_note-193)

The provincial cabinet is then appointed by the Chief Minister who takes the [Oath of office](/wiki/Oath_of_office "Oath of office") from the Governor.[[183]](#cite_note-194) In matters of civil administration, the [Chief Secretary](/wiki/Chief_Secretary_(Pakistan) "Chief Secretary (Pakistan)") assists the Chief Minister on executing its right to ensure the writ of the government and the constitution.[[165]](#cite_note-A_Brief_History_of_Pakistan-170)[[184]](#cite_note-195)

### Judicial branch

Main article: [Peshawar High Court](/wiki/Peshawar_High_Court "Peshawar High Court")

The [Peshawar High Court](/wiki/Peshawar_High_Court "Peshawar High Court") is the province's [highest court](/wiki/High_Court "High Court") of law whose judges are appointed by the approval of the [Supreme Judicial Council](/wiki/Supreme_Judicial_Council_of_Pakistan "Supreme Judicial Council of Pakistan") in [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), interpreting the laws and overturn those they find unconstitutional.

## Major cities

Main article: [List of cities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa_by_population "List of cities in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa by population")

[Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") is the capital and largest city of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. The city is the most populous and comprises more than one-eighth of the province's population.

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Economy_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Economy of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/27/Forestry_by_Province.jpg/250px-Forestry_by_Province.jpg)](/wiki/File:Forestry_by_Province.jpg)

Khyber Pakhtunkhwa's dominance: forestry

Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has the [third largest](/wiki/List_of_Pakistani_provinces_by_GDP "List of Pakistani provinces by GDP") provincial economy in Pakistan. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa's share of Pakistan's GDP has historically comprised 10.5%, although the province accounts for 11.9% of Pakistan's total population. The part of the economy that Khyber Pakhtunkhwa dominates is forestry, where its share has historically ranged from a low of 34.9% to a high of 81%, giving an average of 61.56%.[[185]](#cite_note-196) Currently, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa accounts for 10% of Pakistan's GDP,[[186]](#cite_note-197) 20% of Pakistan's mining output[[187]](#cite_note-198) and, since 1972, it has seen its economy grow in size by 3.6 times.[[188]](#cite_note-199)

Agriculture remains important and the main cash crops include wheat, maize, tobacco (in Swabi), rice, sugar beets, as well as fruits are grown in the province.

Some manufacturing and high-tech investments in Peshawar have helped improve job prospects for many locals, while trade in the province involves nearly every product. The bazaars in the province are renowned throughout Pakistan. Unemployment has been reduced due to the establishment of industrial zones.

Workshops throughout the province support the manufacture of small arms and weapons. The province accounts for at least 78% of the [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") production in Pakistan.[[189]](#cite_note-200)

## Infrastructure

See also: [Provincial Highways of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Provincial_Highways_of_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Provincial Highways of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa")

The Sharmai Hydropower Project is a proposed power generation project located in the Upper Dir District of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa on the [Panjkora River](/wiki/Panjkora_River "Panjkora River") with an installed capacity of 150MW.[[190]](#cite_note-201)

## Social issues

The [Awami National Party](/wiki/Awami_National_Party "Awami National Party") sought to rename the province "Pakhtunkhwa", which translates to "Land of Pakhtuns" in the [Pashto language](/wiki/Pashto_language "Pashto language").[[191]](#cite_note-202) This was opposed by some non-Pashtuns in the province and political parties such as the [Pakistan Muslim League-N](/wiki/Pakistan_Muslim_League-N "Pakistan Muslim League-N") (PML-N) and [Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal](/wiki/Muttahida_Majlis-e-Amal "Muttahida Majlis-e-Amal") (MMA), due to the PML-N deriving its support in the province from primarily non-Pashtun [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_people "Hazara people") regions.

In 2010, the announcement that the province would have a new name led to a wave of protests in the Hazara region.[[192]](#cite_note-203) On 15 April 2010, [Pakistan's senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Pakistan "Senate of Pakistan") officially named the province "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa" with 80 senators in favour and 12 opposed.[[193]](#cite_note-rename-204) The MMA, who until the elections of 2008 had a majority in the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa government, had proposed "Afghania" as a compromise name.[[194]](#cite_note-205)

After the [2008 general election](/wiki/2008_Pakistani_general_election "2008 Pakistani general election"), the Awami National Party formed a coalition provincial government with the [Pakistan Peoples Party](/wiki/Pakistan_Peoples_Party "Pakistan Peoples Party").[[195]](#cite_note-206) The Awami National Party has its strongholds in the Pashtun areas of Pakistan, particularly in the Peshawar valley, while [Karachi](/wiki/Karachi "Karachi") in [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") has one of the largest Pashtun populations in the world—around 7 million by some estimates.[[196]](#cite_note-207) In the 2008 election, the ANP won two Sindh assembly seats in Karachi. The Awami National Party has been instrumental in fighting the Taliban. In [the 2013 general election](/wiki/2013_Pakistani_general_election "2013 Pakistani general election") Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf won a majority in the [provincial assembly](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa_Assembly "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Assembly") and has now formed their government in coalition with [Jamaat-e-Islami Pakistan](/wiki/Jamaat-e-Islami_Pakistan "Jamaat-e-Islami Pakistan").[[197]](#cite_note-208)

## Folk music and culture

See also: [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa clothing](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa_clothing "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa clothing") and [Peshawari chappal](/wiki/Peshawari_chappal "Peshawari chappal")

### Music

Pashto folk music is popular in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa and has a rich tradition going back hundreds of years. The main instruments are the [rubab](/wiki/Rubab_(instrument) "Rubab (instrument)"), [mangey](/w/index.php?title=Mangey&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mangey (page does not exist)") and [harmonium](/wiki/Harmonium_(hand-pumped) "Harmonium (hand-pumped)"). Khowar folk music is popular in Chitral and northern Swat. The tunes of Khowar music are very different from those of Pashto, and the main instrument is the Chitrali sitar. A form of band music composed of clarinets (Surnai) and drums is popular in Chitral. It is played at polo matches and dances. The same form of band music is played in the neighbouring [Northern Areas](/wiki/Northern_Areas "Northern Areas").[[200]](#cite_note-211)

### Literature

There is an important literature produced in the province, mainly in Pashto but also in Urdu and in Hindko, and in 2022 more than 25,000 books were published in these three languages.[[201]](#cite_note-212)

As [FATA](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") became part of KPK, all the literacy rates are recalculated by combining literacy rates of both regions.

| Year | Total | Male | Female | Rural | Urban |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1972 | 14.05% | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 1981 | 14.75% | 23.39 | 5.54% | ... | ... |
| 1998[[202]](#cite_note-213) | 32.63% | 47.85% | 16.38% | ... | ... |
| 2017[[203]](#cite_note-214)[[204]](#cite_note-215) | 49.51% | 64.53% | 34.34% | 46.01% | 66.63% |
| 2023[[205]](#cite_note-216) | 51.09% | 64.57% | 37.15% | 48.35% | 65.55% |

Sources:[[206]](#cite_note-217)[[207]](#cite_note-literacy-218)[[208]](#cite_note-startuppakistan.com.pk-219)[[209]](#cite_note-propakistani.pk-220)

Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has traditionally had a very low literacy rate, although this is changing in recent times. As of the 2017 census, the literacy rate for Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (including FATA) is 51.66%. In rural areas, the literacy rate is 48.44% of the population while in urban areas it is 66.86%. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has a huge gap in literacy rate between sexes – for men it is 66.67% while the female literacy rate is 34.58%, just over half the male literacy rate. This gap is particularly prominent in the overwhelmingly-Pashto rural areas, where traditional gender norms have generally limited education of women. As of 2021, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KP) has the highest literacy growth rate in the whole country ([Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")).[[208]](#cite_note-startuppakistan.com.pk-219)[[209]](#cite_note-propakistani.pk-220)

## Sports

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket_(sport) "Cricket (sport)") is the main sport played in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. It has produced world-class sportsmen like [Shahid Afridi](/wiki/Shahid_Afridi "Shahid Afridi"), [Younis Khan](/wiki/Younis_Khan "Younis Khan"), [Khushdil Shah](/wiki/Khushdil_Shah "Khushdil Shah"), [Fakhar Zaman](/wiki/Fakhar_Zaman_(cricketer) "Fakhar Zaman (cricketer)"), [Naseem Shah](/wiki/Naseem_Shah "Naseem Shah") and [Umar Gul](/wiki/Umar_Gul "Umar Gul"). Besides producing cricket players, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa has the honour of being the birthplace of many world-class [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)") players, including greats like Hashim Khan, Qamar Zaman, [Jahangir Khan](/wiki/Jahangir_Khan "Jahangir Khan") and [Jansher Khan](/wiki/Jansher_Khan "Jansher Khan"). [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Includes speakers of [Western Punjabi (Lahnda)](/wiki/Western_Punjabi_(Lahnda) "Western Punjabi (Lahnda)") ([Hindko](/wiki/Hindko "Hindko")-[Saraiki](/wiki/Saraiki_language "Saraiki language"): [Peshawari](/wiki/Peshawari_dialect "Peshawari dialect"), [Pahari-Pothwari](/wiki/Pahari-Pothwari "Pahari-Pothwari"), [Derawali](/wiki/Derawali_dialect "Derawali dialect"), Chinawab, [Multani](/wiki/Multani_(language) "Multani (language)"), [Chachhi](/wiki/Chachhi_dialect "Chachhi dialect"), [Ghebi](/wiki/Ghebi "Ghebi"), [Tanoli](/wiki/Tanoli "Tanoli"), [Kohati](/wiki/Kohati "Kohati"), [Awankari](/wiki/Awankari_dialect "Awankari dialect"), Hariani, and others) and also includes speakers of [Standard Punjabi](/wiki/Standard_Punjabi "Standard Punjabi"). Both are included as one on table for continuity between censuses, as enumeration results frequently shifted as standardization of the various Punjabi dialects and languages was an ongoing process, with speakers of Standard Punjabi comprising a majority as per the 1911 census, while speakers of Western Punjabi (Lahnda) comprised a majority as per the 1921 and 1931 census.

9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-population_180-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-population_180-1) Total responses during all colonial–era (1855, 1868, 1881, 1891, 1901, 1911, 1921, 1931, and 1941) census counts are an amalgamation of all the districts of [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") at the time, including [Hazara](/wiki/Hazara_District "Hazara District"), [Mardan](/wiki/Mardan_District "Mardan District"), [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar_District "Peshawar District"), [Kohat](/wiki/Kohat_District "Kohat District"), [Bannu](/wiki/Bannu_District "Bannu District"), and [Dera Ismail Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ismail_Khan_District "Dera Ismail Khan District"). Religious affiliation was not enumerated in the [Federally Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") and former [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") of [Amb](/wiki/Amb_(princely_state) "Amb (princely state)"), [Chitral](/wiki/Chitral_(princely_state) "Chitral (princely state)"), [Dir](/wiki/Dir_(princely_state) "Dir (princely state)"), [Phulra](/wiki/Phulra "Phulra") and [Swat](/wiki/Swat_(princely_state) "Swat (princely state)") during all colonial–era census counts.  
     
   Religious affiliation was not enumerated in the [Federally Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas") during one modern–era census count (1972). Total responses in all other modern–era (1951, 1961, 1981, 1998, 2017, 2023) census counts are an amalgamation of the [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province") and [Federally Administered Tribal Areas](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas"). Both administrative divisions would ultimately merge to form Khyber Pakhtunkhwa in 2018.

Naval Institute Press. p. 153. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-1612513096](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-1612513096 "Special:BookSources/978-1612513096").
101. **[^](#cite_ref-103)** Haroon, Sana (2008). "The Rise of Deobandi Islam in the North-West Frontier Province and Its Implications in Colonial India and Pakistan 1914–1996". *Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society*. **18** (1): 55. [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [27755911](https://www.jstor.org/stable/27755911). "The stance of the central JUH was pro-Congress, and accordingly the JUS supported the Congressite Khudai Khidmatgars through to the elections of 1937. However the secular stance of Ghaffar Khan, leader of the Khudai Khidmatgars, disparaging the role of religion in government and social leadership, was driving a wedge between the ulama of the JUS and the Khudai Khidmatgars, irrespective of the commitments of mutual support between the JUH and Congress leaderships. In trying to highlight the separateness and vulnerability of Muslims in a religiously diverse public space, the directives of the NWFP ulama began to veer away from simple religious injunctions to take on a communalist tone. The ulama highlighted 'threats' posed by Hindus to Muslims in the province. Accusations of improper behaviour and molestation of Muslim women were levelled against 'Hindu shopkeepers' in Nowshera. Sermons given by two JUS-connected maulvis in Nowshera declared the Hindus the 'enemies' of Islam and Muslims. Posters were distributed in the city warning Muslims not to buy or consume food prepared and sold by Hindus in the bazaars. In 1936, a Hindu girl was abducted by a Muslim in Bannu and then married to him. The government demanded the girl's return, But popular Muslim opinion, supported by a resolution passed by the Jamiyatul Ulama Bannu, demanded that she stay, stating that she had come of her free will, had converted to Islam, and was now lawfully married and had to remain with her husband. Government efforts to retrieve the girl led to accusations of the government being anti-Muslim and of encouraging apostasy, and so stirred up strong anti-Hindu sentiment across the majority Muslim NWFP."
102. **[^](#cite_ref-104)** Haroon, Sana (2008). "The Rise of Deobandi Islam in the North-West Frontier Province and Its Implications in Colonial India and Pakistan 1914–1996". *Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society*. **18** (1): 57–58. [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [27755911](https://www.jstor.org/stable/27755911). "By 1947 the majority of NWFP ulama supported the Muslim League idea of Pakistan. Because of the now long-standing relations between JUS ulama and the Muslim League, and the strong communalist tone in the NWFP, the move away from the pro-Congress and anti-Pakistan party line of the central JUH to interest and participation in the creation of Pakistan by the NWFP Deobandis was not a dramatic one."
